questions.q: Two paths $\gamma_0,\gamma_1:[0,1]\to X$ with the same endpoints are homotopic rel endpoints if:
choices: there exists a continuous $H:[0,1]^2\to X$ fixing endpoints with $H(t,0)=\gamma_0(t),\ H(t,1)=\gamma_1(t)$
they have equal winding number in every space
questions.explain: Homotopy rel endpoints is exactly a continuous deformation $H$ through paths with endpoints fixed for all deformation times.

questions.q: How many boundary points does the interval $[0,1]$ have?
questions.explain: As a manifold with boundary, $[0,1]$ has boundary $\{0,1\}$, so there are $2$ boundary points.

questions.q: On the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, compute $\gamma(1/4)$ as $a+bi$.

hard.q: Path concatenation $\alpha*\beta$ is associative up to homotopy rel endpoints. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing it fails on the nose (not just up to homotopy)?
choices: $(\alpha*\beta)*\gamma$ and $\alpha*(\beta*\gamma)$ always differ by a non-continuous reparametrisation
$(\alpha*\beta)*\gamma$ traverses $\alpha$ on $[0,1/4]$ while $\alpha*(\beta*\gamma)$ traverses $\alpha$ on $[0,1/2]$, so they are unequal as functions $[0,1]\to X$
Concatenation is strictly associative whenever $X$ is a metric space
Associativity fails only for loops, not for paths with distinct endpoints
hard.explain: $(\alpha*\beta)*\gamma$ spends the first quarter of $[0,1]$ on $\alpha$, the second on $\beta$, and the last half on $\gamma$, whereas $\alpha*(\beta*\gamma)$ spends the first half on $\alpha$. The functions are genuinely different even though they are homotopic rel endpoints via a linear reparametrisation homotopy.

hard.q: Let $\gamma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{C}^*$ be a loop with $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$. The homotopy class of $\gamma$ in $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*,1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ equals what integer?
hard.explain: $\gamma$ winds around the origin $4\pi/2\pi=2$ full times, so its winding number is $2$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in proving that the path-homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation on paths from $x_0$ to $x_1$?
choices: Reflexivity (constant homotopy) → Symmetry (reparametrise $s\mapsto 1-s$) → Transitivity (concatenate homotopies in the $s$-direction)
hard.explain: Standard proof order: first the constant homotopy $H(t,s)=\gamma(t)$ gives reflexivity; reversing the deformation parameter gives symmetry; stacking $H_1(t,s)$ on $[0,1/2]$ and $H_2(t,2s-1)$ on $[1/2,1]$ in $s$ gives transitivity.

questions.q: Select every space that is simply connected.
questions.hint: Simply connected means path-connected with $\pi_1=0$. Convex/contractible Euclidean spaces are simply connected; spheres $S^n$ are simply connected for $n\ge 2$; circles, punctured planes, and tori carry non-contractible loops.
questions.explain: $\mathbb R^n$ is contractible, hence simply connected. $S^2$ has $\pi_1=0$ (every loop contracts; $S^n$ is simply connected for $n\ge 2$). $S^1$ has $\pi_1\cong\mathbb Z$ (winding number). $\mathbb R^2\setminus\{0\}$ deformation-retracts onto $S^1$, so $\pi_1\cong\mathbb Z$. $T^2$ has $\pi_1\cong\mathbb Z^2$.

questions.q: What is the rank of $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$ as an abelian group?
questions.explain: $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)=0$, the trivial group, so its rank is $0$.
A full turn on the unit circle lands back at $1$: $e^{2\pi i}=1+0i$.

hard.q: $S^2$ is simply connected. Which argument correctly shows every loop $\gamma:S^1\to S^2$ is null-homotopic?
choices: Every loop on $S^2$ misses at least one point, and $S^2$ minus a point is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^2$, which is contractible
The Euler characteristic of $S^2$ is $2$, so all loops are trivial
$S^2$ has no holes, so loops must be constant
Every map from a compact space to $S^2$ is null-homotopic
hard.hint: Reduce to a plane by using that loops miss at least one point.
hard.explain: A space-filling loop would require $\gamma$ to be surjective; by a small transversality/smooth perturbation argument every smooth loop misses a point. Removing that point gives $\mathbb{R}^2$, and any loop in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contracts to a point via the straight-line homotopy, so $\gamma$ was null-homotopic in $S^2$.

hard.q: Which of these spaces is NOT simply connected?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{R}^3$ minus a line is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$ (retract radially onto a circle linking the line), so $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}\neq 0$. The other three spaces are all simply connected.

hard.q: The van Kampen theorem applied to $S^2 = U \cup V$ (each a contractible open hemisphere with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$) gives $\pi_1(S^2)\cong$ the trivial group. How many generators does $\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)$ have after the amalgamation kills all generators?
hard.explain: $\pi_1(U)=\pi_1(V)=0$, so the amalgamated free product $0*_{\mathbb{Z}}0$ forces the single generator of $\pi_1(U\cap V)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to map to $0$ in both factors, yielding the trivial group with $0$ generators.

questions.explain: Loops on $S^1$ are classified by winding number, giving an isomorphism with $\mathbb{Z}$.

questions.q: If a loop winds $-3$ times around $S^1$, what integer does it represent in $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$?
questions.explain: The winding number itself is the class in $\mathbb{Z}$.

hard.q: By van Kampen's theorem, $\pi_1(T^2)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. Which decomposition is used?
choices: Write $T^2=S^1\vee S^1$ and apply the free-product formula
Write $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ and use the product formula $\pi_1(X\times Y)\cong\pi_1(X)\times\pi_1(Y)$
Triangulate $T^2$ with $2$ triangles and read off the presentation
Apply excision to the pair $(T^2, S^1)$
hard.explain: $\pi_1$ preserves products: $\pi_1(S^1\times S^1)\cong\pi_1(S^1)\times\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. The two generators correspond to the meridian and longitude loops.

hard.q: The Klein bottle $K$ has $\pi_1(K)=\langle a,b\mid abab^{-1}=1\rangle$. This group is non-abelian. Which element witnesses non-commutativity?
choices: $a$ and $b$ commute because $ab=ba$ follows from the relation
$ba\ne ab$: from $aba=b$ one gets $ab=b a^{-1}b^{-1}b=ba^{-1}$, which equals $ba$ only if $a=a^{-1}$
All groups with two generators are abelian
The relation $abab^{-1}=1$ forces $b$ to be the identity
hard.explain: Rewrite the relation as $ab=ba^{-1}$. This means $a$ and $b$ do NOT commute (unless $a^2=1$), so the group is non-abelian; it is an HNN extension of $\mathbb{Z}$ by $\mathbb{Z}$.

hard.q: Let $X=\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{p_1,p_2\}$ (the plane with two punctures). The rank of $\pi_1(X)$ as a free group equals how many?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{R}^2$ minus $n$ points deformation-retracts onto a wedge of $n$ circles, so $\pi_1\cong F_n$. For $n=2$ the free group $F_2$ has rank $2$.

questions.q: Under the exponential covering $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, what is the fibre $p^{-1}(*)$ over the basepoint $*=1$?
questions.explain: $e^{2\pi i t}=1$ exactly when $t\in\mathbb{Z}$, so the fibre over the basepoint is the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}\subset\mathbb{R}$ — the discrete set whose elements label the sheets of the cover.

questions.q: Lift the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$ to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ starting at $0$. What integer winding number $\tilde\gamma(1)$ do you get?
questions.explain: The unique lift through $0$ is $\tilde\gamma(t)=2t$, since $p(2t)=e^{2\pi i (2t)}=e^{4\pi i t}=\gamma(t)$. Its endpoint is $\tilde\gamma(1)=2$, so the loop winds twice.

questions.q: Which property of $\mathbb{R}$ makes $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ the *universal* cover of $S^1$ (rather than just *some* cover)?
questions.hint: "Universal" means initial among connected covers — what does that require of the total space's $\pi_1$?
questions.explain: A connected cover is universal precisely when its total space is simply connected ($\pi_1=0$). $\mathbb{R}$ is contractible, hence simply connected, so $\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ dominates every other connected cover via a unique factoring map.

questions.q: By the Galois correspondence for covering spaces, connected covers of a (suitable) base $X$ biject with conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1(X)\cong\mathbb Z$, match each cover with the subgroup $H\le\mathbb Z$ to which it corresponds.
questions.hint: Cover sheets equal $[\pi_1(X):H]$. Universal cover ↔ trivial subgroup, $n$-sheeted ↔ index-$n$ subgroup, trivial cover ↔ full group.
questions.explain: Galois theory of covering spaces: covers of $X$ correspond to subgroups of $\pi_1(X)$, with sheet count equal to index. For $X=S^1$, $\pi_1=\mathbb Z$. The universal cover $\mathbb R\to S^1$ has fiber $\mathbb Z$ over each point — corresponds to trivial subgroup $\{0\}$. The $n$-sheeted cover $z\mapsto z^n$ corresponds to $n\mathbb Z$ (index $n$). The identity is the cover for $\mathbb Z$ itself (index 1).

questions.q: For $p_n:S^1\to S^1$, $p_n(z)=z^n$, how many points lie over a generic point of the target circle when $n=5$?
questions.explain: The map $z\mapsto z^n$ is an $n$-sheeted cover, so generic fibers have $n$ points.

questions.q: Under $p_2(z)=z^2$, one preimage of $-1$ is $i$. Give the other preimage as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: Solutions to $z^2=-1$ are $\pm i$. If one is $i$, the other is $-i$.

hard.q: The covering space correspondence says connected covers of $X$ (up to isomorphism) correspond to conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which cover corresponds to the subgroup $3\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$?
choices: There is no cover corresponding to $3\mathbb{Z}$
hard.explain: The subgroup $n\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$ has index $n$ and corresponds to the $n$-sheeted cyclic cover $z\mapsto z^n$. For $n=3$ this is $p_3$, whose deck transformation group is $\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$.

hard.q: The universal cover of $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ is $\mathbb{R}^2$. The deck transformation group acts by integer translations. How many deck transformations map the origin $(0,0)$ to the point $(2,3)$?
hard.explain: Deck transformations of a regular cover act freely and transitively on each fiber. There is exactly $1$ deck transformation sending any chosen fiber point to any other given fiber point.

hard.q: Path lifting: given a covering $p:\tilde{X}\to X$, a path $\gamma:[0,1]\to X$, and a lift $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0\in p^{-1}(\gamma(0))$, how many lifts $\tilde{\gamma}$ exist?
choices: None in general — lifting may fail
Exactly one, by the unique path-lifting property
One for each sheet of the cover
hard.explain: The unique path-lifting property of covering spaces guarantees exactly one lift $\tilde{\gamma}:[0,1]\to\tilde{X}$ with $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0$ and $p\circ\tilde{\gamma}=\gamma$.

questions.q: Arrange the steps to set up the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence for a space $X = A\cup B$ with $A,B$ open.
items: Form the short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to C_*(A\cap B)\xrightarrow{\alpha}C_*(A)\oplus C_*(B)\xrightarrow{\beta}C_*(A)+C_*(B)\to 0$, where $\alpha(c)=(c,-c)$ and $\beta(a,b)=a+b$.
Apply the snake lemma / zig-zag lemma to extract the long exact sequence in homology: $\cdots\to H_n(A\cap B)\to H_n(A)\oplus H_n(B)\to H_n(X)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)\to\cdots$
Verify the sequence of chain complexes is exact: $\beta\alpha=0$, $\ker\beta=\operatorname{im}\alpha$, and $\beta$ is surjective onto $C_*(A)+C_*(B)$.
Use the small-simplices theorem (barycentric subdivision) to identify $H_n(C_*(A)+C_*(B))$ with $H_n(X)$.
questions.hint: Build the SES of chain complexes, verify exactness, identify the third term with $C_*(X)$ via subdivision, then take homology.
questions.explain: Mayer–Vietoris flow: (a) build the SES of chain complexes from the inclusion data, (b) check exactness, (c) replace the third term $C_*(A)+C_*(B)$ by $C_*(X)$ in homology — content of the small-simplices theorem (every chain in $X$ is homologous to one supported in $A$ or $B$), (d) the snake lemma converts the SES of chain complexes into the LES in homology with connecting morphism $\partial_*\colon H_n(X)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$.

questions.q: Using reduced homology ranks for a torus $T^2$ ($\beta_0,\beta_1,\beta_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute $\chi(T^2)=\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2$.

hard.q: A continuous map $f:X\to Y$ induces $f_*:H_n(X)\to H_n(Y)$. If $f$ is homotopic to a constant map, which is true for all $n\ge 1$?
hard.explain: Homotopy invariance: homotopic maps induce the same map on $H_n$. A constant map factors through a point, whose reduced homology vanishes in degree $\ge 1$, so $f_*=0$ there.

hard.q: Use Mayer–Vietoris on $S^2=U\cup V$ with $U,V$ contractible open sets and $U\cap V\simeq S^1$ to compute $\chi(S^2)$ from $\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)$. Recall $\chi(\mathrm{pt})=1$ and $\chi(S^1)=0$.
hard.explain: Inclusion–exclusion for Euler characteristic: $\chi(S^2)=\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)=1+1-0=2$, matching $\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2=1-0+1$.
